content.text: Why Text-to-Video Is Harder Than Text-to-Image
Generating a single image from text is a spatial problem — pixels must be arranged coherently. Video adds a third constraint:
. Every frame must look plausible on its own, and every consecutive pair must depict smooth, physically believable motion. Three challenges compound the difficulty:
: a character's face, clothing, and the scene background must not flicker between frames.
: the model must learn that cloth ripples, fire flickers, and water flows — not just that they exist.
: a 10-second 720p clip at 24 fps has 240 frames. Diffusing all of them jointly is computationally prohibitive without compression.
Two architectural families tackle these constraints differently:
graft temporal attention onto a proven image diffusion backbone, while
(Video DiTs) treat space and time as a unified patch grid from the start.

attrs.alt: Text-to-Video architecture families: Temporal U-Net (SVD/ModelScope) vs Video DiT (Sora/CogVideoX), with model comparison table

content.text: Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) and ModelScopeT2V both extend a pre-trained image U-Net by inserting

between (or after) the spatial convolution blocks. The spatial weights are initialised from a text-to-image checkpoint and optionally frozen during early training, which lets the model inherit strong image priors.

At inference time all F frames are stacked along a new batch-like axis and processed simultaneously. Each temporal attention head attends across the F-frame axis so that every frame can borrow context from its neighbours:

# Pseudo-code for a U-Net block with temporal attention
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TemporalUNetBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, channels, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.spatial_conv = nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1)
        self.spatial_norm = nn.GroupNorm(32, channels)
        # Temporal attention: sequence length = F (number of frames)
        self.temporal_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(channels, num_heads, batch_first=True)
        self.temporal_norm = nn.LayerNorm(channels)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (B*F, C, H, W)  — frames concatenated along batch dim
        BF, C, H, W = x.shape
        B = BF // self.F  # split batch and frame dims

        # Spatial branch (standard 2-D conv)
        h = self.spatial_norm(self.spatial_conv(x) + x)

        # Reshape for temporal attention: (B, F, C*H*W) → reduce spatial
        # In practice spatial features are pooled to tokens first;
        # for clarity we do per-pixel temporal attn on a (B*H*W, F, C) view.
        h = h.permute(0, 2, 3, 1)          # (BF, H, W, C)
        h = h.reshape(B, self.F, H * W, C)
        h = h.permute(0, 2, 1, 3).reshape(B * H * W, self.F, C)
        attn_out, _ = self.temporal_attn(h, h, h)
        h = self.temporal_norm(h + attn_out)
        h = h.reshape(B, H * W, self.F, C).permute(0, 2, 1, 3)
        h = h.reshape(B * self.F, H, W, C).permute(0, 3, 1, 2)
        return h
The latent space is still the 2-D VAE from Stable Diffusion (4-channel, 8× downsampled), so a 576×1024 frame compresses to a 72×128 latent. For 25 frames the diffusion model sees a tensor of shape
— manageable on a single high-memory GPU.
, takes a conditioning image plus motion-bucket-id (controls motion intensity) and generates 25 frames:

from diffusers import StableVideoDiffusionPipeline
from diffusers.utils import load_image, export_to_video
import torch

pipe = StableVideoDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "stabilityai/stable-video-diffusion-img2vid-xt",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    variant="fp16",
)
pipe.to("cuda")
pipe.enable_model_cpu_offload()  # keeps VRAM under ~12 GB

image = load_image("starting_frame.png").resize((1024, 576))

frames = pipe(
    image,
    decode_chunk_size=8,        # decode 8 frames at a time to save VRAM
    num_frames=25,
    motion_bucket_id=127,       # 0 = static, 255 = lots of motion
    fps=7,
    noise_aug_strength=0.02,    # small noise helps generalization
    generator=torch.manual_seed(42),
).frames[0]

export_to_video(frames, "output.mp4", fps=7)
Architecture B: Video Diffusion Transformer (Sora, CogVideoX)
Video DiTs discard the convolutional U-Net and instead tokenise video as a 3-D grid of

. A patch covers a small spatial tile (e.g. 2×2 pixels in latent space) across a short temporal span (e.g. 2 consecutive frames). Every patch becomes one sequence token, giving the transformer a global view of the entire clip.
that compresses the video along both spatial and temporal axes simultaneously. Typical compression ratios: 4× spatial (each axis) and 4× temporal, so a 49-frame 720p clip becomes a latent of shape
— a 16× reduction in token count compared to frame-by-frame 2D VAE encoding. This is what makes long clips tractable.
Standard RoPE assigns 1-D positions. Video DiTs use
with three separate frequency groups — one per axis (height, width, time). This lets the model generalise to variable resolutions and frame counts without retraining.

Each transformer block applies full 3-D self-attention (every token attends to every other token, across all spatial positions and frames), followed by cross-attention to text tokens from a T5-XXL encoder, and
conditioning (timestep and classifier-free guidance scale are injected via learned scale + shift into every layer norm).
CogVideoX-5B is the best publicly available open-weight text-to-video model as of 2024, licensed Apache-2.0:

# pip install diffusers transformers accelerate
import torch
from diffusers import CogVideoXPipeline
from diffusers.utils import export_to_video

pipe = CogVideoXPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "THUDM/CogVideoX-5b",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)
pipe.enable_sequential_cpu_offload()  # ~16 GB VRAM otherwise
pipe.vae.enable_tiling()              # tile the 3D VAE to save memory

prompt = (
    "A time-lapse of a sunflower field at golden hour, "
    "petals swaying gently in the breeze, warm cinematic lighting."
)

# CogVideoX-5B: 49 frames @ 8 fps → ~6 seconds
video_frames = pipe(
    prompt=prompt,
    num_videos_per_prompt=1,
    num_inference_steps=50,
    num_frames=49,
    guidance_scale=6,
    generator=torch.Generator("cpu").manual_seed(42),
).frames[0]

export_to_video(video_frames, "cogvideox_output.mp4", fps=8)
print(f"Generated {len(video_frames)} frames")
ModelScopeT2V is a lighter Temporal U-Net model (256×256, 16 frames) useful for quick experiments and fine-tuning. It integrates directly with Hugging Face Diffusers:

from diffusers import DiffusionPipeline
from diffusers.utils import export_to_video
import torch

pipe = DiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "ali-vilab/text-to-video-ms-1.7b",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    variant="fp16",
)
pipe.to("cuda")

video_frames = pipe(
    "A tabby cat playing with a ball of yarn",
    num_inference_steps=25,
    num_frames=16,
).frames[0]

export_to_video(video_frames, "modelscope_output.mp4", fps=8)
CFG works the same way as in image diffusion — the model runs twice per step, once conditioned on the text prompt and once unconditionally (empty text), and the outputs are extrapolated:

is the guidance scale (typically 6–9 for video). Higher guidance increases prompt adherence but reduces diversity and can cause over-saturation. For video, both the temporal and spatial quality degrade faster with very high guidance than they do for images, so typical video guidance scales are lower than typical image scales (~7.5).
The standard metric for text-to-video generation is
, an extension of FID that uses a video understanding network (I3D, pre-trained on Kinetics-400) to extract temporal features from both generated and real videos:
are the mean and covariance of I3D features over real (r) and generated (g) clips. Lower FVD is better. A practical FVD implementation:

# pip install torch-fidelity  (or use the open-source fvd repo)
# This shows the conceptual API; real I3D loading is omitted for brevity.
import numpy as np
from scipy import linalg

def frechet_distance(mu_real, sigma_real, mu_gen, sigma_gen, eps=1e-6):
    """
    FVD (or FID) core computation.
    mu_*, sigma_*: statistics computed from I3D embeddings of video clips.
    """
    diff = mu_real - mu_gen
    # Stable matrix square root via eigendecomposition
    covmean, _ = linalg.sqrtm(sigma_real @ sigma_gen, disp=False)
    if np.iscomplexobj(covmean):
        covmean = covmean.real  # numerical noise on near-singular matrices
    fvd = diff @ diff + np.trace(sigma_real + sigma_gen - 2 * covmean)
    return float(fvd)

def compute_fvd(real_clips, gen_clips, i3d_model):
    """
    real_clips, gen_clips: (N, T, H, W, C) uint8 arrays, values 0-255.
    i3d_model: pretrained I3D that returns (N, 400) feature vectors.
    """
    def embed(clips):
        # Resize to 224×224, normalise, extract penultimate layer
        clips_t = preprocess_for_i3d(clips)  # (N, 3, T, 224, 224)
        with torch.no_grad():
            feats = i3d_model(clips_t).cpu().numpy()  # (N, 400)
        return feats

    f_real = embed(real_clips)
    f_gen  = embed(gen_clips)
    mu_r, sig_r = f_real.mean(0), np.cov(f_real, rowvar=False)
    mu_g, sig_g = f_gen.mean(0),  np.cov(f_gen,  rowvar=False)
    return frechet_distance(mu_r, sig_r, mu_g, sig_g)
State-of-the-art open models achieve FVD ≈ 300–500 on UCF-101 (lower bound set by real videos ≈ 0). Commercial models like Sora and Kling are not publicly evaluated on standardised benchmarks.

Temporal U-Nets benefit from the large body of pre-trained image diffusion weights and strong community fine-tuning ecosystems. They are faster to train for short clips and are more memory-efficient because spatial and temporal dimensions are handled at different resolutions in the U-Net hierarchy.

Video DiTs scale better with model size and compute. Full 3-D self-attention lets every token directly influence every other token — a global receptive field from the first layer. This produces more globally coherent scene structure and better adherence to complex prompts, at the cost of quadratic memory in sequence length. Techniques like FlashAttention-2, ring attention (across GPUs), and 3D VAE compression are necessary at production scales.
Fine-tuning CogVideoX-5B on a custom video dataset requires video-caption pairs. The recommended approach mirrors DreamBooth for images:

# Skeleton — actual training loop omitted; see CogVideoX training scripts.
from diffusers import CogVideoXDPMScheduler, CogVideoXTransformer3DModel
from diffusers.training_utils import compute_snr
import torch

# Dataset: list of {"video": tensor (T,H,W,3), "caption": str}
# Pre-encode videos with the 3D VAE once to save GPU time during training.

def encode_video(vae, video_tensor):
    """video_tensor: (1, C, T, H, W) float in [-1, 1]"""
    with torch.no_grad():
        latent = vae.encode(video_tensor).latent_dist.sample()
    return latent * vae.config.scaling_factor

# Training loop (condensed)
for step, batch in enumerate(train_dataloader):
    latents = encode_video(vae, batch["pixel_values"])
    noise = torch.randn_like(latents)
    bsz = latents.shape[0]
    timesteps = torch.randint(0, scheduler.config.num_train_timesteps,
                              (bsz,), device=latents.device)
    noisy_latents = scheduler.add_noise(latents, noise, timesteps)
    # Text encoder: T5-XXL
    text_embeds = encode_prompt(batch["captions"])
    # Transformer forward pass
    noise_pred = transformer(
        noisy_latents,
        encoder_hidden_states=text_embeds,
        timestep=timesteps,
    ).sample
    # v-prediction or epsilon prediction depending on scheduler config
    target = scheduler.get_velocity(latents, noise, timesteps)
    loss = torch.nn.functional.mse_loss(noise_pred, target)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
Temporal U-Nets (SVD, ModelScope) extend image diffusion with temporal attention layers and inherit image priors — practical for ≤25 frames.
Video DiTs (Sora, CogVideoX) treat video as spacetime patch tokens with 3-D self-attention and 3-D VAE compression — superior quality and scalability for longer clips.
CogVideoX-5B is the strongest open-weight model available: DiT architecture, 3D VAE, Apache-2.0 license, native diffusers support.
FVD (Fréchet Video Distance) is the standard evaluation metric — lower is better, computed over I3D features on real vs generated clips.
3D RoPE and variable-length patchification allow Video DiTs to handle arbitrary resolutions and durations at test time without retraining.
CFG guidance scales for video (≈6–9) are lower than for images to avoid temporal flickering artifacts caused by aggressive extrapolation.
